old_claim.note: Codex (Early Book Form) is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency is retained because libraries predate codices and can be based on tablets, scrolls, or other written media.

source_shape.source_locator: What is the Library of Ashurbanipal section describes over 30,000 cuneiform tablets from Nineveh under Ashurbanipal, who ruled 669-c.631 BCE.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The British Museum anchors a major library collection in seventh-century BCE Assyria, long before the codex form became common.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A library can be an organized tablet or scroll collection; requiring codices incorrectly narrows libraries to later bound-book institutions.

ontology_before: The library node treated the codex as a historical predecessor, implying bound books were prerequisite for library institutions.
ontology_after: The library node remains connected to writing, philosophy, and construction, but no longer requires the later codex format.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Invariant changed: Libraries must not directly depend on codex unless a narrower bound-book library node is introduced.
would_reject_if: A source shows this Libraries node is intentionally scoped only to codex-based libraries.
The graph splits tablet archives from libraries and keeps this node only for later book libraries.
A source demonstrates that the Ashurbanipal collection should not be treated as a library-like institution.
short_reason: Libraries existed as tablet collections before the codex and should not depend on bound books.
